text: This is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English.
zh: 这里是BBC英语教学的《六分钟英语》节目。

text: I'm Neil. And I'm Beth. In Britain we call them crisps and you might know them as potato chips.
zh: 我是尼尔。我是贝丝。在英国我们把它叫做crisps,你可能更熟悉potato chips这个说法。

text: But whatever you call them, these fried potato slices are probably the world's favourite snack.
zh: 但不管你怎么称呼它,这种油炸薯片大概是全世界最受欢迎的零食了。

text: Neil, are you a big crisp eater and what's your favourite flavour?
zh: 尼尔,你很爱吃薯片吗?你最喜欢什么口味?

text: I love crisps, Beth. They are my favourite snack.
zh: 贝丝,我超爱吃薯片,它们是我最爱的零食。

text: My favourite flavour is prawn cocktail, which is controversial because a lot of people don't like that flavour.
zh: 我最喜欢的口味是鸡尾酒虾味,不过这个口味挺有争议的,因为很多人并不喜欢。

text: I think my favourite is salt and vinegar. Yummy. You're making me hungry.
zh: 我觉得我最喜欢的是盐醋味。真好吃,你说得我都饿了。

text: Well crisps might be popular, but with lots of oil and salt, they're not the healthiest food.
zh: 虽然薯片很受欢迎,但因为含油和盐都很多,它们其实并不算健康食品。

text: So why do we love them so much? That's what BBC World Service programme The Food chain wanted to find out.
zh: 那我们为什么这么爱吃薯片呢?这正是BBC世界服务节目《The Food Chain》想要探究的问题。

text: Here, presenter Ruth Alexander talks to Mitchell Dingwall, a food executive responsible for developing new crisp flavours.
zh: 在节目中,主持人露丝·亚历山大采访了负责研发新口味薯片的食品行业高管米切尔·丁沃尔。

text: Why do we love crisps so much? I think it's just that crunch, right?
zh: 我们为什么这么爱吃薯片呢?我觉得就是那种咔嚓声,对吧?

text: You can't beat the crunch and then that delicious salty feeling in your mouth and it just becomes a snowball effect.
zh: 没什么能比得上那种脆响,再加上嘴里那种美味的咸香感,就形成了滚雪球效应。

text: You just want more of it. For Mitchell, the best thing about crisps is the loud crunch.
zh: 你会越吃越想吃。对米切尔来说,薯片最棒的地方就是那响亮的咔嚓声。

text: He says you can't beat it, – an idiom meaning something like, that's the best thing and you won't find anything better.
zh: 他说“you can't beat it”——这是一个习语,意思是这是最好的,你找不到比它更好的了。

text: Mitchell says once you've started eating crisps, it's hard to stop and you just want to eat more and more.
zh: 米切尔说,一旦开始吃薯片就很难停下来,你会越吃越想吃。

text: He calls this a snowball effect, a situation where doing something causes other similar things to
zh: 他把这称为“滚雪球效应”,指做一件事引发一系列类似的事情。

text: happen. You eat one crisp and before you know it, the whole packet's gone.
zh: 你吃了一片薯片,不知不觉整包就吃完了。

text: In this episode, we'll be visiting two countries in love with crisps.
zh: 在这一期节目中,我们将走访两个热爱薯片的国家。

text: And as usual, we'll be learning some useful new words and phrases,
zh: 一如往常,我们也会学习一些实用的新单词和短语,

text: all of which you can find on our website, bbclearningenglish .com. But first, I have a question for you, Beth.
zh: 这些内容都可以在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上找到。不过首先,我有个问题要问贝丝。

text: The record for the world's largest packet of crisps was recently broken in Saudi Arabia, but how much did the packet weigh?
zh: 世界上最大袋薯片的纪录最近在沙特阿拉伯被打破了,那么这袋薯片有多重呢?

text: Was it... a about 1 .5 tonnes, b about 2 .5 tonnes,
zh: 是a,大约1.5吨,还是b,大约2.5吨,

text: or c about 3 .5 tonnes? OK, wow, they are all huge.
zh: 或者c,大约3.5吨?好吧,哇,这几个数字都好大。

text: I'm going to guess about 3 .5 tonnes. OK, we'll find out the answer later.
zh: 我猜大约是3.5吨。好,我们稍后揭晓答案。

text: Let's travel first to Singapore, home of Jolene Ng, a market research analyst who specialises in salty
zh: 我们先去新加坡看看,那里有一位专攻咸味零食的市场调研分析师乔琳·吴。

text: snacks. Here's Jolene talking with Ruth Alexander for BBC programme The Food Chain.
zh: 下面是乔琳与露丝·亚历山大在BBC节目《The Food Chain》中的对话。

text: Is the potato chip the most popular of all salty snacks? It's very, very much dependent on personal preference.
zh: 薯片是所有咸味零食中最受欢迎的吗?这在很大程度上取决于个人喜好。

text: But if I were to look at certain countries, you could see like in the UK, it's very much part and parcel of life.
zh: 但如果看某些国家,比如在英国,薯片几乎是生活中不可或缺的一部分。

text: You don't have it only during snack times. You have it with lunch as well.
zh: 不只是零食时间才吃,午餐时也会吃。

text: You put it, my husband's from UK and he kind of put it in his sandwich as well.
zh: 我丈夫是英国人,他甚至会把薯片夹进三明治里。

text: Oh yeah, a cheese sandwich with a crisp inside.
zh: 哦对,芝士三明治里夹一片薯片。

text: Oh yeah, that's great. Exactly. Jolene says the popularity of different snacks depends on personal preference.
zh: 哦对,那样超棒。没错。乔琳说不同零食的受欢迎程度取决于个人偏好。

text: Someone's subjective liking or disliking for something based on their personal opinion.
zh: 也就是一个人基于自己观点而产生的主观喜好或厌恶。

text: In some places, crisps are part and parcel of everyday life.
zh: 在有些地方,薯片是日常生活中不可或缺的一部分。

text: Part and parcel means to be an essential element or feature of something.
zh: “part and parcel”的意思是某事物中不可或缺的重要组成部分。

text: In Britain, crisps are so essential that people even eat crisp sandwiches.
zh: 在英国,薯片是如此不可或缺,以至于人们甚至会吃薯片三明治。

text: But not everywhere is so in love with potato chips.
zh: 但并不是每个地方都这么热爱薯片。

text: Let's meet Jenny Martinez, a food blogger from Mexico.
zh: 让我们认识一下珍妮·马丁内斯,一位来自墨西哥的美食博主。

text: In Mexico, tortilla corn chips are eaten instead of potato chips,
zh: 在墨西哥,人们吃的是玉米片而不是薯片,

text: and it's common to see street vendors selling bags of corn tortillas filled with different
zh: 常见街头小贩售卖装满各种酱料的玉米片袋子。

text: sauces. Here, Jenny tells Ruth Alexander more for BBC World Service programme The Food Chain.
zh: 下面,珍妮为BBC世界服务节目《The Food Chain》向露丝·亚历山大做了更多介绍。

text: For you, which is your go -to snack, the tortilla chip or the potato chip?
zh: 对你来说,你最常吃的零食是玉米片还是薯片?

text: Definitely the tortilla chip. Why? Because you could do so many things.
zh: 当然是玉米片。为什么呢?因为你可以用它做很多花样。

text: Dip it in almost everything. Add in some guacamole, spicy salsa
zh: 几乎什么都能蘸着吃。加点鳄梨酱、辣莎莎酱,

text: or just sour cream with a mixture of chipotle sauce.
zh: 或者只是酸奶油加一点奇波特辣酱调味。

text: Ruth asks if tortillas are Jenny's go -to snack.
zh: 露丝问珍妮最常吃的零食是不是玉米片。

text: You can use the phrase go -to to describe something which is relied upon to be the best thing for a particular purpose.
zh: 你可以用“go-to”这个短语来形容某样东西是完成某个特定目的时最可靠、最常用的选择。

text: For example, for a lot of people, Wikipedia is the go -to website for online information.
zh: 举个例子,对很多人来说,维基百科是获取网上信息的首选网站。

text: And Jenny thinks corn tortillas are especially good with guacamole, a paste made of mashed avocado,
zh: 珍妮认为玉米片配鳄梨酱特别好吃,鳄梨酱是一种由捣碎的牛油果制成的酱料,

text: which sounds delicious and may be a little healthier than a British crisp sandwich.
zh: 听起来很美味,而且可能比英式薯片三明治要健康一点。

text: Speaking of which, what was the answer to your question, Neil?
zh: 说到这个,尼尔,你那个问题的答案是什么呢?

text: I asked you how heavy the world's largest bag of crisps was.
zh: 我问你世界上最大的一袋薯片有多重。

text: And I said very heavy – over three and a Well, it's not that big,
zh: 我说非常重——超过三吨半。其实并没有那么大,

text: but the real answer is still enormous – about 1 .5 tonnes.
zh: 但真正的答案依然十分惊人——大约1.5吨。

text: The bag, made in Riyadh, was 9 metres tall.
zh: 这袋薯片是在利雅得制作的,高达9米。

text: OK, let's recap the vocabulary we've learned, starting with the phrase, you can't beat that,
zh: 好,我们来回顾一下今天学到的词汇,首先是短语“you can't beat that”,

text: meaning that something is the best and you won't find anything better.
zh: 意思是某样东西是最好的,你找不到更好的了。

text: A snowball effect is a situation where one action causes other similar actions to happen.
zh: “滚雪球效应”指的是一个动作引发一系列类似动作的情况。

text: Someone's personal preference is their subjective liking or disliking for something based on their own opinion.
zh: 一个人的“personal preference”指的是基于自身观点而产生的主观喜好或厌恶。

text: To be part and parcel of something means to be an essential or integral feature of it.
zh: “part and parcel”意思是某事物中不可或缺、密不可分的一部分。

text: And something which is described as the go -to can be relied upon as the best thing for a particular situation.
zh: 被称为“go-to”的东西,指的是在特定情况下可以依赖的最佳选择。

text: And finally, guacamole is a paste made of mashed avocado, lime juice and salt.
zh: 最后,“guacamole”是一种由捣碎的牛油果、青柠汁和盐制成的酱料。

text: Delicious! delicious. Once again, our six minutes are up, but if you're hungry for more,
zh: 太美味了!没错,太美味了。我们的六分钟又到时间了,不过如果你还意犹未尽,

text: then head over to our website bbclearningenglish .com where you'll find a worksheet and quiz for this episode.
zh: 欢迎访问我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com,那里有本期节目的练习题和小测验。

text: See you again soon, but for now, Goodbye.
zh: 我们下次再见,现在先说再见啦。

text: Six Minute English, from bbclearningenglish .com
zh: 《六分钟英语》,来自bbclearningenglish.com
